<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 240 | image1 = Sri Mariamman Temple Singapore 2 amk.jpg | alt1 = Krishna | width1 = 1964 | height1 = 2620 | image2 = Skanda at Miaoying Temple.JPG | alt2 = Skanda | width2 = 1200 | height2 = 1600 | image3 = Gold beaker, Inca, 1450-1532 AD, southern Peru - Staatliches Museum für Völkerkunde München - DSC08504.JPG | alt3 = Beaker | image4 = Musée africain Lyon 130909 01.jpg | alt4 = Orisha | image5 = Ares Canope Villa Adriana b.jpg | alt5 = Ares | image6 = Itzamna e Ixchel.JPG | alt6 = Itzamna e Ixchel | width7 = 340 | height7 = 260 | image8 = Spas vsederzhitel sinay.jpg | alt8 = Jesus Christ | width9 = 340 | height9 = | image10 = Maestro dei mesi, 01 giano bifronte (anno vecchio e anno nuovo) gennaio, 1225-1230 ca. 03.JPG | alt10 = Janus | width10 = 340 | height10 = 260 | image11 = Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png | alt11 = Kami | width11 = 400 | height11 = 190 | footer = Examples of representations of deities across different cultures. Clockwise from top left: [[:en:Hinduism|Hinduism]], [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], [[:en:Yoruba religion|Yoruba]], [[:en:Maya religion|Maya]], [[:en:Religion in ancient Rome|Ancient Rome]], [[Shinto]], [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], [[:en:Ancient Greek religion|Greek]], and [[:en:Religion in the Inca Empire|Inca]]. | align = | direction = | caption1 = | caption2 = }} {{Ruby-ja|'''Gods'''|かみがみ}} are supernatural beings considered to possess divinity and sacredness.<ref name="OBrien">{{cite book|last1=O'Brien|first1=Jodi|title=Encyclopedia of Gender and Society|year=2009|publisher=Sage|location=Los Angeles|isbn=978-1-4129-0916-7|page=191|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=_nyHS4WyUKEC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> The [[:en:Oxford English Dictionary|Oxford English Dictionary]] defines "deity" as "a god or goddess in polytheistic religions" and as something revered like a god.<ref name="Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=Angus|title=Oxford Dictionary of English|year=2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-957112-3|page=461|edition=3rd|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=anecAQAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> [[:en:C. Scott Littleton|C. Scott Littleton]] defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. [[:en:Monotheism|Monotheism]] recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while [[:en:Polytheism|Polytheism]] worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Henotheism|Henotheism]] recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Nontheistic religion|Nontheistic religion]]s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], [[:en:Egyptians|Egyptians]], [[:en:Greeks|Greeks]], [[:en:Romans|Romans]], [[:en:Norse|Norse]]) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}
== See Also == {{Portal|Religion}} * [[:en:Deification|Deification]] * [[:en:Deicide|Deicide]] * [[:en:Hero cult|Hero cult]] * [[:en:Imperial cult|Imperial cult]] * [[:en:List of deities|List of deities]] * [[:en:List of deities in fiction|Fictional deities]] * [[:en:Odinism|Odinism]]
{{translated page|ja|神々|version=96782346|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Deities]]
[[Category:Completion tier 3]]
}}
[[ja:神々]]

Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], [[:en:Egyptians|Egyptians]], [[:en:Greeks|Greeks]], [[:en:Romans|Romans]], [[:en:Norse|Norse]]) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 240 | image1 = Sri Mariamman Temple Singapore 2 amk.jpg | alt1 = Krishna | width1 = 1964 | height1 = 2620 | image2 = Skanda at Miaoying Temple.JPG | alt2 = Skanda | width2 = 1200 | height2 = 1600 | image3 = Gold beaker, Inca, 1450-1532 AD, southern Peru - Staatliches Museum für Völkerkunde München - DSC08504.JPG | alt3 = Beaker | image4 = Musée africain Lyon 130909 01.jpg | alt4 = Orisha | image5 = Ares Canope Villa Adriana b.jpg | alt5 = Ares | image6 = Itzamna e Ixchel.JPG | alt6 = Itzamna e Ixchel | width7 = 340 | height7 = 260 | image8 = Spas vsederzhitel sinay.jpg | alt8 = Jesus Christ | width9 = 340 | height9 = | image10 = Maestro dei mesi, 01 giano bifronte (anno vecchio e anno nuovo) gennaio, 1225-1230 ca. 03.JPG | alt10 = Janus | width10 = 340 | height10 = 260 | image11 = Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png | alt11 = Kami | width11 = 400 | height11 = 190 | footer = Examples of representations of deities across different cultures. Clockwise from top left: [[:en:Hinduism|Hinduism]], [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], [[:en:Yoruba religion|Yoruba]], [[:en:Maya religion|Maya]], [[:en:Religion in ancient Rome|Ancient Rome]], [[Shinto]], [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], [[:en:Ancient Greek religion|Greek]], and [[:en:Religion in the Inca Empire|Inca]]. | align = | direction = | caption1 = | caption2 = }} {{Ruby-ja|'''Gods'''|かみがみ}} are supernatural beings considered to possess divinity and sacredness.<ref name="OBrien">{{cite book|last1=O'Brien|first1=Jodi|title=Encyclopedia of Gender and Society|year=2009|publisher=Sage|location=Los Angeles|isbn=978-1-4129-0916-7|page=191|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=_nyHS4WyUKEC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> The [[:en:Oxford English Dictionary|Oxford English Dictionary]] defines "deity" as "a god or goddess in polytheistic religions" and as something revered like a god.<ref name="Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=Angus|title=Oxford Dictionary of English|year=2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-957112-3|page=461|edition=3rd|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=anecAQAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> [[:en:C. Scott Littleton|C. Scott Littleton]] defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. [[:en:Monotheism|Monotheism]] recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while [[:en:Polytheism|Polytheism]] worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Henotheism|Henotheism]] recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Nontheistic religion|Nontheistic religion]]s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], [[:en:Egyptians|Egyptians]], [[:en:Greeks|Greeks]], [[:en:Romans|Romans]], [[:en:Norse|Norse]]) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}

Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. [[:en:Monotheism|Monotheism]] recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while [[:en:Polytheism|Polytheism]] worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Henotheism|Henotheism]] recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Nontheistic religion|Nontheistic religion]]s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], [[:en:Egyptians|Egyptians]], [[:en:Greeks|Greeks]], [[:en:Romans|Romans]], [[:en:Norse|Norse]]) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}
== See Also == {{Portal|Religion}} * [[:en:Deification|Deification]] * [[:en:Deicide|Deicide]] * [[:en:Hero cult|Hero cult]] * [[:en:Imperial cult|Imperial cult]] * [[:en:List of deities|List of deities]] * [[:en:List of deities in fiction|Fictional deities]] * [[:en:Odinism|Odinism]]
{{translated page|ja|神々|version=96782346|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[ja:神々]]

Scott Littleton|C. Scott Littleton]] defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. [[:en:Monotheism|Monotheism]] recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while [[:en:Polytheism|Polytheism]] worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Henotheism|Henotheism]] recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Nontheistic religion|Nontheistic religion]]s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], [[:en:Egyptians|Egyptians]], [[:en:Greeks|Greeks]], [[:en:Romans|Romans]], [[:en:Norse|Norse]]) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神々]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125122761]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 240 | image1 = Sri Mariamman Temple Singapore 2 amk.jpg | alt1 = Krishna | width1 = 1964 | height1 = 2620 | image2 = Skanda at Miaoying Temple.JPG | alt2 = Skanda | width2 = 1200 | height2 = 1600 | image3 = Gold beaker, Inca, 1450-1532 AD, southern Peru - Staatliches Museum für Völkerkunde München - DSC08504.JPG | alt3 = Beaker | image4 = Musée africain Lyon 130909 01.jpg | alt4 = Orisha | image5 = Ares Canope Villa Adriana b.jpg | alt5 = Ares | image6 = Itzamna e Ixchel.JPG | alt6 = Itzamna e Ixchel | width7 = 340 | height7 = 260 | image8 = Spas vsederzhitel sinay.jpg | alt8 = Jesus Christ | width9 = 340 | height9 = | image10 = Maestro dei mesi, 01 giano bifronte (anno vecchio e anno nuovo) gennaio, 1225-1230 ca. 03.JPG | alt10 = Janus | width10 = 340 | height10 = 260 | image11 = Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png | alt11 = Kami | width11 = 400 | height11 = 190 | footer = Examples of representations of deities across different cultures. Clockwise from top left: {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|WD=Q9089|lt=Hinduism}}, {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, {{ill|Yoruba religion|en|Yoruba religion|WD=Q1086440|lt=Yoruba}}, {{ill|Maya religion|en|Maya religion|WD=Q1921707|lt=Maya}}, {{ill|Religion in ancient Rome|en|Religion in ancient Rome|WD=Q337547|lt=Ancient Rome}}, [[Shinto]], {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, {{ill|Ancient Greek religion|en|Ancient Greek religion|WD=Q855270|lt=Greek}}, and {{ill|Religion in the Inca Empire|en|Religion in the Inca Empire|WD=Q3238302|lt=Inca}}. | align = | direction = | caption1 = | caption2 = }} {{Ruby-ja|'''Gods'''|かみがみ}} are supernatural beings considered to possess divinity and sacredness.<ref name="OBrien">{{cite book|last1=O'Brien|first1=Jodi|title=Encyclopedia of Gender and Society|year=2009|publisher=Sage|location=Los Angeles|isbn=978-1-4129-0916-7|page=191|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=_nyHS4WyUKEC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> The {{ill|Oxford English Dictionary|en|Oxford English Dictionary|WD=Q44996|lt=Oxford English Dictionary}} defines "deity" as "a god or goddess in polytheistic religions" and as something revered like a god.<ref name="Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=Angus|title=Oxford Dictionary of English|year=2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-957112-3|page=461|edition=3rd|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=anecAQAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> {{ill|C. Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|WD=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}
== See Also == {{Portal|Religion}} * [[:en:Deification|Deification]] * {{ill|Deicide|en|Deicide|WD=Q1855439|lt=Deicide}} * [[:en:Hero cult|Hero cult]] * {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|WD=Q3271557|lt=Imperial cult}} * [[:en:List of deities|List of deities]] * [[:en:List of deities in fiction|Fictional deities]] * {{ill|Odinism|en|Odinism|WD=Q7077824|lt=Odinism}}
{{translated page|ja|神々|version=96782346|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:神々]]

Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|WD=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}
== See Also == {{Portal|Religion}} * [[:en:Deification|Deification]] * {{ill|Deicide|en|Deicide|WD=Q1855439|lt=Deicide}} * [[:en:Hero cult|Hero cult]] * {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|WD=Q3271557|lt=Imperial cult}} * [[:en:List of deities|List of deities]] * [[:en:List of deities in fiction|Fictional deities]] * {{ill|Odinism|en|Odinism|WD=Q7077824|lt=Odinism}}
{{translated page|ja|神々|version=96782346|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:神々]]

{{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 240 | image1 = Sri Mariamman Temple Singapore 2 amk.jpg | alt1 = Krishna | width1 = 1964 | height1 = 2620 | image2 = Skanda at Miaoying Temple.JPG | alt2 = Skanda | width2 = 1200 | height2 = 1600 | image3 = Gold beaker, Inca, 1450-1532 AD, southern Peru - Staatliches Museum für Völkerkunde München - DSC08504.JPG | alt3 = Beaker | image4 = Musée africain Lyon 130909 01.jpg | alt4 = Orisha | image5 = Ares Canope Villa Adriana b.jpg | alt5 = Ares | image6 = Itzamna e Ixchel.JPG | alt6 = Itzamna e Ixchel | width7 = 340 | height7 = 260 | image8 = Spas vsederzhitel sinay.jpg | alt8 = Jesus Christ | width9 = 340 | height9 = | image10 = Maestro dei mesi, 01 giano bifronte (anno vecchio e anno nuovo) gennaio, 1225-1230 ca. 03.JPG | alt10 = Janus | width10 = 340 | height10 = 260 | image11 = Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png | alt11 = Kami | width11 = 400 | height11 = 190 | footer = Examples of representations of deities across different cultures. Clockwise from top left: {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|WD=Q9089|lt=Hinduism}}, {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, {{ill|Yoruba religion|en|Yoruba religion|WD=Q1086440|lt=Yoruba}}, {{ill|Maya religion|en|Maya religion|WD=Q1921707|lt=Maya}}, {{ill|Religion in ancient Rome|en|Religion in ancient Rome|WD=Q337547|lt=Ancient Rome}}, [[Shinto]], {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, {{ill|Ancient Greek religion|en|Ancient Greek religion|WD=Q855270|lt=Greek}}, and {{ill|Religion in the Inca Empire|en|Religion in the Inca Empire|WD=Q3238302|lt=Inca}}. | align = | direction = | caption1 = | caption2 = }} {{Ruby-ja|'''Gods'''|かみがみ}} are supernatural beings considered to possess divinity and sacredness.<ref name="OBrien">{{cite book|last1=O'Brien|first1=Jodi|title=Encyclopedia of Gender and Society|year=2009|publisher=Sage|location=Los Angeles|isbn=978-1-4129-0916-7|page=191|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=_nyHS4WyUKEC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> The {{ill|Oxford English Dictionary|en|Oxford English Dictionary|WD=Q44996|lt=Oxford English Dictionary}} defines "deity" as "a god or goddess in polytheistic religions" and as something revered like a god.<ref name="Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=Angus|title=Oxford Dictionary of English|year=2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-957112-3|page=461|edition=3rd|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=anecAQAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> {{ill|C. Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|WD=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 240 | image1 = Sri Mariamman Temple Singapore 2 amk.jpg | alt1 = Krishna | width1 = 1964 | height1 = 2620 | image2 = Skanda at Miaoying Temple.JPG | alt2 = Skanda | width2 = 1200 | height2 = 1600 | image3 = Gold beaker, Inca, 1450-1532 AD, southern Peru - Staatliches Museum für Völkerkunde München - DSC08504.JPG | alt3 = Beaker | image4 = Musée africain Lyon 130909 01.jpg | alt4 = Orisha | image5 = Ares Canope Villa Adriana b.jpg | alt5 = Ares | image6 = Itzamna e Ixchel.JPG | alt6 = Itzamna e Ixchel | width7 = 340 | height7 = 260 | image8 = Spas vsederzhitel sinay.jpg | alt8 = Jesus Christ | width9 = 340 | height9 = | image10 = Maestro dei mesi, 01 giano bifronte (anno vecchio e anno nuovo) gennaio, 1225-1230 ca. 03.JPG | alt10 = Janus | width10 = 340 | height10 = 260 | image11 = Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png | alt11 = Kami | width11 = 400 | height11 = 190 | footer = Examples of representations of deities across different cultures. Clockwise from top left: {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|WD=Q9089|lt=Hinduism|1=Hinduism}}, {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, {{ill|Yoruba religion|en|Yoruba religion|WD=Q1086440|lt=Yoruba|1=Yoruba religion}}, {{ill|Maya religion|en|Maya religion|WD=Q1921707|lt=Maya|1=Maya religion}}, {{ill|Religion in ancient Rome|en|Religion in ancient Rome|WD=Q337547|lt=Ancient Rome|1=Religion in ancient Rome}}, [[Shinto]], {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity|1=Christianity}}, {{ill|Ancient Greek religion|en|Ancient Greek religion|WD=Q855270|lt=Greek|1=Ancient Greek religion}}, and {{ill|Religion in the Inca Empire|en|Religion in the Inca Empire|WD=Q3238302|lt=Inca|1=Religion in the Inca Empire}}. | align = | direction = | caption1 = | caption2 = }} {{Ruby-ja|'''Gods'''|かみがみ}} are supernatural beings considered to possess divinity and sacredness.<ref name="OBrien">{{cite book|last1=O'Brien|first1=Jodi|title=Encyclopedia of Gender and Society|year=2009|publisher=Sage|location=Los Angeles|isbn=978-1-4129-0916-7|page=191|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=_nyHS4WyUKEC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> The {{ill|Oxford English Dictionary|en|Oxford English Dictionary|WD=Q44996|lt=Oxford English Dictionary|1=Oxford English Dictionary}} defines "deity" as "a god or goddess in polytheistic religions" and as something revered like a god.<ref name="Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=Angus|title=Oxford Dictionary of English|year=2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-957112-3|page=461|edition=3rd|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=anecAQAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> {{ill|C. Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|WD=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton|1=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism|1=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism|1=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion|1=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians|1=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks|1=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans|1=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse|1=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}
== See Also == {{Portal|Religion}} * [[:en:Deification|Deification]] * {{ill|Deicide|en|Deicide|WD=Q1855439|lt=Deicide|1=Deicide}} * [[:en:Hero cult|Hero cult]] * {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|WD=Q3271557|lt=Imperial cult|1=Imperial cult}} * [[:en:List of deities|List of deities]] * [[:en:List of deities in fiction|Fictional deities]] * {{ill|Odinism|en|Odinism|WD=Q7077824|lt=Odinism|1=Odinism}}

Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|WD=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton|1=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism|1=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism|1=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion|1=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians|1=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks|1=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans|1=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse|1=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.

Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|WD=Q9159|lt=Monotheism|1=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|WD=Q150395|lt=Henotheism|1=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|WD=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion|1=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|WD=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians|1=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|WD=Q539051|lt=Greeks|1=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|WD=Q299368|lt=Romans|1=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|WD=Q909583|lt=Norse|1=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
== See Also == {{Reflist |refs = <ref name="OBrien2">{{cite book |last = O'Brien |first = Julia M. |title = Oxford Encyclopedia of the Bible and Gender Studies |year=2014 |publisher=Oxford University Press, Incorporated |isbn=978-0-19-983699-4 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = ZU-nBAAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Bullivant">{{cite book |last1=Bullivant |first1=Stephen |last2=Ruse |first2=Michael |title=The Oxford Handbook of Atheism |year=2013 |publisher=Oxford University Publishing |isbn=978-0-19-964465-0 |url = {{Google books |plainurl = y |id = jbIVAgAAQBAJ }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language = en }}</ref> <ref name="Keown">{{cite book |last=Keown |first=Damien |title=Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction |year = 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |isbn = 978-0-19-966383-5 |edition=New |url = {{Google books |plainurl=y |id=_QXX0Uq29aoC }} |accessdate=22 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> }}

Scott Littleton|en|C. Scott Littleton|qid=Q3001846|lt=C. Scott Littleton|1=C. Scott Littleton}} defined gods as beings with greater powers than humans, engaging with humans either positively or negatively, often elevating them to new levels of awareness.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Littleton |first1=C. Scott|title=Gods, Goddesses, and Mythology|year=2005|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|location=New York|isbn=978-0-7614-7559-0|page=378|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=3ufSStXPECkC |page=378 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref> Deities may be described as male gods or female goddesses.
Religions are categorized by the number of deities they worship. {{ill|Monotheism|en|Monotheism|qid=Q9159|lt=Monotheism|1=Monotheism}} recognizes only one deity (often referred to simply as "God")<ref>{{cite book|last1=Becking|first1=Bob|last2=Dijkstra|first2=Meindert|last3=Korpel| first3=Marjo|last4=Vriezen |first4=Karel|title=Only One God?: Monotheism in Ancient Israel and the Veneration of the Goddess Asherah|date=2001|publisher=New York|location=London|isbn=978-0-567-23212-0|page=189|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=eMneBAAAQBAJ|page=189}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>, while {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|qid=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}} worships multiple deities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Jeannine K.|title=Scripture as Communication: Introducing Biblical Hermeneutics|date=2007|publisher=Baker Academic|isbn=978-0-8010-2788-8|page=72|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=5QJjyGoxEzkC}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Henotheism|en|Henotheism|qid=Q150395|lt=Henotheism|1=Henotheism}} recognizes a supreme deity but does not deny the existence of others, viewing them as facets of the same divine principle.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Taliaferro|first1=Charles|last2=Harrison|first2=Victoria S.|last3=Goetz|first3=Stewart|title=The Routledge Companion to Theism|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-33823-6|pages=78–79|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=ct7fCgAAQBAJ}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name=ross73>{{cite book|last1=Reat|first1=N. Ross|last2=Perry|first2=Edmund F.|title=A World Theology: The Central Spiritual Reality of Humankind|date=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-33159-3|pages=73–75|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=vD2TJNc7NE4C}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref> {{ill|Nontheistic religion|en|Nontheistic religion|qid=Q6508891|lt=Nontheistic religion|1=Nontheistic religion}}s reject supreme eternal creator gods but accept pantheons of deities who may live, die, and reincarnate.<ref name="Keown"/>{{rp|35–37}}<ref name="Bullivant"/>{{rp|357–58}}.
Historically, many ancient cultures (e.g., [[:en:Mesopotamians|Mesopotamians]], {{ill|Egyptians|en|Egyptians|qid=Q1061510|lt=Egyptians|1=Egyptians}}, {{ill|Greeks|en|Greeks|qid=Q539051|lt=Greeks|1=Greeks}}, {{ill|Romans|en|Romans|qid=Q299368|lt=Romans|1=Romans}}, {{ill|Norse|en|Norse|qid=Q909583|lt=Norse|1=Norse}}) personified natural phenomena with intentional causality.<ref name="Malandra">{{cite book|last1=Malandra|first1=William W.|title=An Introduction to Ancient Iranian Religion: Readings from the Avesta and the Achaemenid Inscriptions|year=1983|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|location=Minneapolis, MN|isbn=978-0-8166-1115-7|pages=9–10|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=nZQMrjukmboC|page=9}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref><ref name="Fløistad">{{cite book|last1=Fløistad|first1=Guttorm|title=Volume 10: Philosophy of Religion|year=2010|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media B.V.|location=Dordrecht|isbn=978-90-481-3527-1|pages=19–20|edition=1st|url={{google books |plainurl=y |id=BclABayC1QQC|page=19}}|accessdate=28 June 2017|language=en}}</ref>. Deities were often seen as both transcendent and immanent, with some cultures imagining them as residing within the senses or intellect of living beings.<ref>{{cite book |last = Potter |first = Karl H. |title = The Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Volume 3: Advaita Vedanta up to Samkara and His Pupils |year=2014|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-5651-0|pages=272–74 |url={{Google books |plainurl=y |id=Ydf_AwAAQBAJ |page=272 }} |accessdate=28 June 2017 |language=en }}</ref>.
